item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Concept 1: your downloafed information is word replaced. awkward.
Concept 2: extension is considered similar to an advert and removed.
Concept 3: enjoy saying horse every time you want to say force

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This may have been asked before, but what would happen if someone used this while a "Word Replacer" extension, such as a "force to horse" one was installed and enabled in their browser? For example, if someone reads an article about Star Wars and "May the Force Be With You," it says "May the Horse Be With You?"

item.title: Re: A certain 22th 'paratech' came to mind
item.content:encoded: Nah, chills. It's not that this skip is that similar to the Copying Toast anyway. In the 22th century paratech's case, the bread is anomalous. Not necessarily so with this skip (the bread involved in this skip is usually not anomalous).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On the other hand, if you word-for-word copied the websites with your answers and they happened to be reasonably well-known studying utilities, you might have to somehow prove that you didn't cheat using mundane means. Which I suppose makes calculus the prime choice for this, since math doesn't exactly need rephrasing.

item.title: Re: A certain 22th 'paratech' came to mind
item.content:encoded: You know, I didn't know Doraemon even existed until Japanese 201 class, so I'm afraid there was no connection.

item.title: A certain 22th 'paratech' came to mind
item.content:encoded: After I happen to look at a Doraemon shirt a kid was wearing, I couldn't help but wonder if 22th century copying toast have anything to do with this skip's method of knowledge delivery when you wrote this XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok then, but what if it was this school?

Notice how their acronym says "SCPS". This could be a Foundation front organization! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, unless your institution has a policy regarding and the means to detect anamolous methods to improve performance. Most schools aren't going to know or care that you pray the morning of the test, wore your lucky socks or summoned the cone of power either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if someone somehow finds out about SCP-2268, manages to get around the redirects, and uses it with a website or two that is relevant to their finals and then has a PB&J sandwich for breakfast using the toast from the disk drive on the morning before their finals? Would it be considered cheating if someone had a "peanut butter, jelly, and calculus sandwich" for breakfast on exam day?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like to imagine that if you look close enough, you can see the entire contents of the downloafed page burnt into the toast.
+1 for making a pun work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So you've…b-read it before?
ill let myself out

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright. A bunch of people have compared the effects of SCP-2268 to those of SCP-1584, our other weird website article. I'd argue that Foundation personnel would look into possibly using SCP-2268 on anomalous websites like 1584 to see if any new insights into how they tick could be gained from the "downloafing". As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Adding the following paragraph to the article's end:

Use of SCP-2268 to study anomalous webpages, including SCP-1584, is currently pending review.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My first impulse is to say that if you do, I'll link it in the article, but I don't know the rules about making websites for SCPs. :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After reading this I have a strange urge to relay make this as site

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: With Dr. Wonderbreadment's Enriching Epistemoloaf! Now you, too, can be free of the Problem(TM) of Knowledge(TM)!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Teaching toast? What next? Didactic donuts? Pedantic pastries? WHERE DOES IT END?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That made me giggle more than the puns did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Insert loaf 22 and press button to continue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The foundation could use this SCP as a way to make mokey :v

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to say, I think this is the only pun on the site that has never failed to make me laugh. It sounds so menacing but in context, it actually works quite well.

item.title: Very Abbreviated Test Logs:
item.content:encoded:
Post your taglines here… I think we can write these in our own heads.

D-100000: "I know Kung fu."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: downloaf*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bread of knowledge. That's all I can say. I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If a Maxwellian needs to downloaf information, something has gone horribly, terribly wrong. Or hilariously right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Or a Maxwellian ;p

But yah, the concerned time frame is exactly during the downloafing process.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Beyond it calling emergency services during downloafing?

The toast doesn't have internet or cellular reception, and unless you're Junior Researcher Beckett from 3900, humans don't have internet or cellular reception either. So only poor Beckett would call emergency services. For a week. Non-stop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And the emergency services? X)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Clearly, the toast floats. (alternate ideas include: life preserver pattern toasted into the bread, hapless toast-eater yields 73.5 newtons of upthrust when submerged in fresh water for a week, 1584 and 2668 creators get together at a nice brunch and high-five each other over a warm croissant)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now (or, rather, about a year ago) that someone mentioned 1584, I'm tempted to imagine what will happen if you're trying to downloaf that XD

On a more serious note, to whoever suggested that one might as well downloaf literally the entire Library of Congress, have you accounted for the (relatively) significant overhead needed to dough one huge page outside of LoC before it can be downloafed into one bread? (I'm assuming that trying to downloaf LoC's homepage, or any one page in it for that matter, won't do you much good.) That might well be the downside needed for that particular shenanigan ^^; actually, forget that argument. If the url of that huge dough spreads, someone else can downloaf your dough without the overhead you had to go thru CX

Here, have my upvote :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
what the fuck

god dammit this is brilliant. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh! Thank you for pointing those out! I did indeed mean what you said, but I'll go ahead and clarify that. I don't know anything about error codes, I appreciate that you pointed that out. I'll change it to a 405 error.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's really silly, but yet I love it. The baguette-shaped progress bar is what got me.

One improvement I can suggest is that in the third sentence of the second paragraph of the description, you suddenly mention the page freezing in a manner that suggests you'd intended to already introduce that. I assume that you mean once you start downloafing the page will no longer respond to user input, but you could clarify that.

Also, from a technical standpoint, error 404 doesn't make sense—it's used to indicate that a client tried to request a resource that doesn't exist on the server. You could try looking at https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_HTTP_status_codes and picking a 4xx that fits more. I'd say probably 405 or 406 fit best: the situation is essentially that the client has made a request that the server could deliver, but the client is unable to process due to insufficient hardware.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It gives you an error and requests that you loaf an actual page. If you do it 42 times in a row it'll close automatically.

The information contained in google.com is the searchbar, whatever the google doodle is, and some other stuff. You'd get that, and only that. And since it only does still images and words, you'd get the still of the google doodle, so you wouldn't even be able to play little flash games in your head while contemplating the mistake of downloafing google.

Your best bet to know everything would be to try and downloaf the entirety of wikipedia, and I'm still not entirely sure what happens if you do that. You can only eat so much bread in a sitting, and you can only hold so much in your brain before things get fucky. But there's a lot of things that make you go insane, or kill you, and frankly I don't think this could be improved by adding "U DIE" or "U GO MAD" to the article. But just saying "you can only downloaf x toast at a time before you stop absorbing information" is kinda flat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What happens if I load the page that I'm on? Like load the anomalous URL from the anomalous URL page? Would I be able to search anything and everything?

Also what happens if I load in google? Does that make me know everything?

Questions aside a solid +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bread bread bread bread! Congrats, Specs. :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Happy anniversary of the bread! It's a year old now! I'm so proud.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Goddamn you, I have to upvote now, the puns bring tears to my eyes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bread and loving it.
Geez.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh wow, I'm on the front page! Bread for all! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not creepy or thought provoking or anything on par with something needing securing and containment for the sake of protecting humanity from its effects.

The Foundation contains all anomalous things, regardless of if they present a threat to humanity or not.

Also uh… we've got a lot of stuff on the mainsite that isn't creepy or inherently thought-provoking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this should have a J designation. I mean I liked it and all. That it was funny and great. But that's the thing, I thought It is was funny and great. Not creepy or thought provoking or anything on par with something needing securing and containment for the sake of protecting humanity from its effects. So I did not vote up or down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure why, but I think this is really cool.
Have an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're lucky the tomatoes got you before I did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So if I were to use this skip on an adult website, would it be safe to say that the end result would be… pornbread?

*beamed by SCP-504*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly yeah this is a good deal like SCP-1584, but frankly, 1584 can't fulfill my burning need for pieces like this on its own, so I'm glad that this article steps up to the plate.

Plus, bread is inherently funny.
Aw yeah. Bread for everyone!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think they have potential, although some tweaking might be needed. One D-class fawning over some cat videos (again, tweaking), or someone creepily looking through somebody's facebook bikini photos. Better yet, give Able, 682, or some other killing machine a picture of cute cat. It would drive them nuts with it stuck in their head all day. A creepypasta keeping some poor sucker up all night? A D-class flaunting his Wikipedia knowledge over the others.

And perhaps raisin bread could be used in a unique way? Or playing a browser game in your head (assuming the storage is small enough)?

Different bread having different storage space? What about programs? An auto delete program? Could they affect the user? Could you install software to your brain?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Often and not, the author usually would be the person to have posted version 0. See History at the bottom of the article.

That said, it might not be always the case (since early articles were moved to Wikidot by non-authors). Anyway, the best way to check is to use jarvis (the chat bot). It will direct you to whoever is deemed the author, if applicable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm the author?

Honestly, I'm not sure what test logs would add to the skip. "Yup, this D-Class now has the Wikipedia page on Terrick Truckle completely memorized. Next!"

(terrick truckle doesn't actually have a wikipedia page, which makes me sad)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are you asking me what I had in mind for test logs? And, tbh, I had a little trouble finding the author, so I decided to ask the user with the highest authority. Sorry about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Toastception.

I mean, technically the website doesn't exactly have a sense of humor, or even sentience, but yeah. Toastception.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now I'm thinking about using this with a website that describes, in poetic detail, the flavor of toast…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey now, I've just been granted power and I fully intend to abuse it! :V

So my question, Tommy1138, is, "What can you do for me?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is quite a non sequitur, since you are replying to TL333s (who is not the author here, and won't be able to grant such a permission).

Instead, it would be more appropriate if you communicate with the author about this. Technically, you already did that, but this might be slightly confusing if you contextualise it as a reply to TL333s' comment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Permission to start writing test logs?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: [citation needed]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Then you'd be bread before you hit the ground.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if you'd reach "triggers sensory overload" before you hit "please insert additional bread"…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think they are complaining that it doesn't have any storage limits, and could downloaf an indefinite amount of information as long as that information was formatted as a single webpage.
